source: Fulbert of Chartres — Letter on Feudal Obligations (On Fealty)
topic: Classic statement of mutual duties in feudal oaths
notes: Fulbert's response to Duke William of Aquitaine; defines what a vassal owes his lord in fealty; crucially introduces *mutuality* — the lord has reciprocal obligations; foundational text for understanding feudal oath theology; brief but dense

source: Anglo-Saxon Form of Oath of Fealty
notes: The actual vernacular oath formula: 'I will be faithful and loyal to N. and love all that he loves and shun all that he shuns...' — the comprehensiveness of the oath (love/shun, not just obey) is theologically significant; predates Norman feudalism and shows the oath tradition has Germanic/Anglo-Saxon roots

source: Fealty — Love Loyalty and Choice in Feudalism
notes: How a person chose their lord — the voluntary and affective dimension of feudal loyalty
access_link: Key point: entering feudal fealty was not typically compelled but chosen; the vassal selected a lord and offered homage voluntarily. This is critical for the oath theology — the oath is binding precisely *because* it was freely entered. Maps onto the 2 Sam 5 structure where the elders *come to David* and covenant with him

notes: Byzantine imperial acclamation ceremony and theological significance
access_link: The emperor was acclaimed by army and senate, then crowned by the Patriarch; the acclamation formula ('axios' — worthy) is the people's recognition of divine appointment, not democratic election. The unction is sacramental: the emperor becomes *christos* (anointed) in a derivative sense. Primary theological source: Eusebius's interpretation of Constantine's sole rule as mirroring divine monarchy

source: English Coronation Oath (Leofric Missal form / 1689 statutory form)
topic: The king's oath to God and people
notes: Three promises: protect the church; forbid robbery and injustice; maintain justice and mercy. Bilateral: people acclaim; king swears to God. UK Parliament briefing SN00435 surveys all historical forms including the Leofric Missal text (c. 973). Parliament PDF now archived at Wayback Machine

source: Fordham Medieval Sourcebook — Feudal Oaths Compilation
topic: Multiple oath of homage and fealty examples
notes: Includes multiple examples; best single online collection of feudal oath primary sources
The emperor as God's viceroy; acclamation by army and senate; anointing; the oath before God — Byzantine synthesis of Hellenistic and Christian monarchy. See also Grokipedia entry above for overview

source: Henry de Bracton — On the Laws and Customs of England
topic: The king under God and law
notes: Key passage vol. 2 p. 33 ll. 8–9: 'Ipse autem rex non debet esse sub homine sed sub deo et sub lege, quia lex facit regem' — 'The king must not be under man but under God and under the law, because law makes the king.' The coronation oath as binding covenant; legal force of royal oaths

topic: Limits on royal power via sworn compact
notes: Even this constraint document is oath-based not election-based; the oath structure underlies constitutional limitation
National covenant before God including political allegiance to the king under God; Reformed political theology of oaths
King swears to uphold law and church; bishops witness; one of the earliest formalized royal oath liturgies in the West

topic: Feudal oath structure in Crusader states
notes: Detailed account of homage and fealty oaths; obligations of king and vassal; breach consequences

source: John Calvin — Institutes of the Christian Religion IV.20
notes: Calvin on civil government; magistrates as God's deputies; the oath of loyalty as morally binding; lesser magistrates

source: GotQuestions.org — What is the Divine Right of Kings?
topic: Scriptural overview of OT monarchy and divine right
notes: Good introductory synthesis tying OT texts to the divine right tradition
